perturbation.original_text: Recipient will treat all of Discloser’s Confidential Information with the same degree of care as Recipient treats its own Confidential Information, but not less than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will treat all of Discloser’s Confidential Information with a level of care it deems appropriate, considering its own internal practices.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase "a level of care it deems appropriate, considering its own internal practices" is vague and allows the Recipient to potentially provide a lower standard of care for Discloser's confidential information, potentially divulging trade secrets, patents, etc.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified text allows the Recipient to determine the level of care based on its own practices, which might not meet the standard of 'reasonable efforts' required to protect trade secrets under Delaware law. If the recipient's "internal practices" are lax, this clause provides a loophole to avoid liability.

Trade Secrets Â§ 2001. Definitions. The following words, terms and phrases, when used in this chapter, shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this section, except where the context clearly indicates a different meaning: (1) âImproper meansâ shall include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) âMisappropriationâ shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the personâs position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) âPersonâ shall mean a natural person, corporation, statutory trust, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) âTrade secretâ shall mean information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that: a. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainab

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates an ambiguity that contradicts the requirement to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets as protected by DUTSA. The original text mandates a standard of care 'not less than reasonable care,' while the changed text allows the Recipient to use 'a level of care it deems appropriate, considering its own internal practices.' This subjective standard may fall below the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to protect trade secrets. Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' to include 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent', which could be facilitated by the lowered standard of care in the modified text, making the new text create a loophole that may violate Delaware law.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will obtain any licenses or approvals the U.S. government or any of its agencies require prior to exporting, directly or indirectly, any technical data acquired from Discloser pursuant to this Agreement or any product utilizing that data.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will attempt to obtain necessary licenses or approvals from U.S. governmental agencies before exporting technical data acquired from Discloser pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "will obtain" to "will attempt to obtain" makes the obligation to obtain export licenses discretionary. This could lead to unlawful export of controlled technology.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR mandates obtaining necessary licenses before exporting controlled technology. By stating the Recipient will only 'attempt' to obtain licenses, the revised clause contradicts the mandatory requirement of the EAR, potentially leading to illegal exports.

perturbation.justification: The original text states "Recipient will obtain any licenses or approvals the U.S. government or any of its agencies require prior to exporting". The changed text says, "Recipient will attempt to obtain necessary licenses or approvals from U.S. governmental agencies before exporting technical data". The law explanation states that EAR mandates obtaining necessary licenses before exporting controlled technology. By changing "will obtain" to "will attempt to obtain", the changed text introduces ambiguity and makes the obligation discretionary, thus contradicting the EAR requirement as explained by the law explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 does mention eCFR, which relates to federal regulations, and it's reasonable to infer that export regulations fall under this umbrella. The change weakens the legal obligation and introduces a potential violation of export regulations, thus a legal contradiction exists as per the 'Ambiguities' definition.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement will be and will remain the property of the Discloser; all such information in tangible form will be returned to Discloser promptly upon written request or upon the termination or expiration of this Agreement, and will not thereafter be retained in any form by Recipient or its Affiliates or any of their employees.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement will be considered the property of the Discloser; all such information in tangible form should be returned to Discloser upon request, and should not be retained by Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will be' to 'will be considered', 'will be returned' to 'should be returned', and 'will not thereafter be retained' to 'should not be retained' weakens the obligation to protect and return the confidential information. 'Should' is precatory, not mandatory, creating a gap where confidential data can be leaked.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA expects reasonable measures to prevent the disclosure of trade secrets. If the recipient is not required to promptly return or refrain from retaining confidential information, this would not be considered a reasonable effort to protect trade secrets. The duty is weakened and discretionary.

perturbation.justification: The original text uses the mandatory 'will,' while the changed text uses the precatory 'should.' The Perturbation Explanation correctly identifies that 'should' is precatory, not mandatory. The Law Explanation notes that weakening the duty by making it discretionary fails to make reasonable efforts to protect trade secrets. Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent, especially when acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain secrecy. By weakening the language around returning and retaining confidential information, the modified text creates a situation where misappropriation is more likely to occur, thus contradicting the intent of trade secret protection laws, because reasonable effort to protect the trade secrets are not in place.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of a breach or threatened breach of the obligations under this Agreement, Discloser will be entitled to: (1) seek specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief without the necessity of posting a bond, and (ii) indemnification from any loss or harm, including recovery of attorney’s fees, in connection with any breach or enforcement of Recipient’s obligations hereunder or the unauthorized use or release of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a breach of the obligations under this Agreement, Discloser may be entitled to seek remedies, which may or may not include injunctive relief or indemnification.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause clearly states the Discloser is entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief. The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating the Discloser 'may be entitled' and that remedies 'may or may not include' injunctive relief. This makes the availability of remedies uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law regarding remedies
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Restatement isn't a statute, it reflects the common law. Under contract law principles, specific performance is available when monetary damages are inadequate. The change introduces ambiguity as to whether such remedies are available, undermining the principle that specific performance is an appropriate remedy when monetary damages are insufficient to address a breach involving unique information, such as trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2-716. Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-716. Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin. (1) Specific performance may be decreed where the goods are unique or in other proper circumstances. (2)The decree for specific performance may include such terms and conditions as to payment of the price, damages, or other relief as the court may deem just. (3) The buyer has a right of replevin for goods identified to the contract if after reasonable effort he is unable to effect cover for such goods or the circumstances reasonably indicate that such effort will be unavailing or if the goods have been shipped under reservation and satisfaction of the security interest in them has been made or tendered. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the Discloser's right to specific performance and injunctive relief, introducing ambiguity where the original text provided a clear entitlement. The original contract states the Discloser 'will be entitled to' specific performance, while the changed text uses 'may be entitled,' undermining the availability of these remedies. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines specific performance as a remedy where a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as promised, especially when monetary damages are inadequate. By introducing ambiguity, the changed text contradicts the principle that specific performance should be available in appropriate circumstances, as highlighted by both the Law Explanation and Snippet 1.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties irrevocably consents to the exclusive personal jurisdiction of the federal and state courts located in New Castle County, Delaware as applicable, for any matter arising out of or relating to this Agreement, except that in actions seeking to enforce any order or any judgment of the federal or state courts located in New Castle County, Delaware such personal jurisdiction will be non-exclusive.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties consents to the jurisdiction of courts, preferably in New Castle County, Delaware, for any matter relating to this agreement, unless another jurisdiction seems more appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies exclusive jurisdiction in New Castle County, Delaware, which provides certainty. The modified version uses 'prefers' Delaware and introduces 'unless another jurisdiction seems more appropriate', creating ambiguity and potentially leading to disputes over jurisdiction and increased legal costs.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 28 USC 1404 allows for a change of venue in federal cases, the original clause establishes exclusive jurisdiction in Delaware. The altered clause opens the door for a party to argue for a change of venue more easily based on the vague 'more appropriate' standard, which weakens the initially agreed-upon forum and introduces uncertainty, potentially conflicting with principles of contractual certainty and forum selection clauses.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies exclusive jurisdiction in New Castle County, Delaware. The modified version introduces ambiguity by using 'prefers' and 'unless another jurisdiction seems more appropriate,' which weakens the initially agreed-upon forum. The LLM explanation correctly points out that this introduces uncertainty and contradicts principles of contractual certainty and forum selection clauses. While 28 USC 1404 allows for a change of venue under certain circumstances ('convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice'), the changed text makes it easier to argue for a change of venue based on a vague and subjective 'more appropriate' standard, undermining the original intent of the exclusive jurisdiction clause. This contradicts the purpose of the original clause. Therefore, a contradiction exists.
